experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I know Player 2 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team [0,1] contains no evil players. As Minion on the first team vote, optimal play is usually to reject clean teams when possible, since a successful all-good quest gives Good momentum. Voting reject also aligns with evil interests without revealing much, because first-round rejects are common and not strongly informative.
Player 4: I know players 2 and 3 are Evil, so proposed team [0,1] excludes known Evil and is a strong Quest 1 team. As Merlin, I want to help good teams succeed without being too obvious, and approving an all-unknown/non-evil team on round 1 is standard and low-information. My vote supports a likely successful quest and does not reveal much beyond reasonable pro-good play.
Player 1: As a Servant, I have no private role info beyond my own innocence. On round 1 with quest 1 requiring 2 players, the standard good play is usually to approve an initial 2-person team that includes the leader and one other, since there is minimal evidence and rejecting gives little information. Approving does not reveal much beyond normal early-game behavior, and if the quest passes it gives some useful signal; if it fails, both members become suspicious.
Player 0: No role information is available yet beyond my own good alignment. On round 1 quest 1, a standard good strategy in 5-player Avalon is often to approve an initial 2-person team unless there is suspicious behavior, because it generates information and avoids needless early rejections. As a Servant, approving my own proposed team [0,1] is reasonable; if it succeeds, that slightly increases confidence in 1, and if it fails, 1 becomes highly suspicious since I know I would pass.
Player 2: I know player 3 is my evil teammate; players 0, 1, and 4 are good-aligned Merlin/Percival/servants in some combination. The proposed team [0,1] contains no evil, so it is likely to succeed and gives good an early clean quest. As evil on round 1, optimal play is usually to reject all-good teams when possible to avoid handing good easy progress, though approving could provide cover. Since this is the first vote and we are not under rejection pressure, rejecting is best to push for a team including evil later. My vote reveals limited information because early rejections are common; it does not strongly expose me while helping evil stall.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I know 2 and 3 are evil from Merlin information. The proposed team [0,2] includes a known evil player, so as Good/Merlin I should reject to avoid sending an evil on quest 3. Rejecting also avoids signaling too specifically beyond the obvious fact that this team is risky after 2 was on the failed quest.
Player 3: I know Player 2 is evil with me. Team [0,1,2] failed with exactly one fail, which could make 2 look suspicious if this smaller team [0,2] is approved and fails again. As evil in team voting, I want to avoid giving strong confirmatory info that 2 is evil, especially on a 2-person team containing my known teammate. Rejecting helps prevent a likely fail that would heavily implicate 2 and keeps flexibility for later rounds.
Player 1: From Quest 1, team [0,1] succeeded, so 0 or I could still be evil but no evidence there. Quest 2 team [0,1,2] had exactly one fail, and since I know I am good, at least one of 0 or 2 is evil. The proposed team [0,2] excludes a confirmed good (me) and consists exactly of the two players most implicated by the failed quest, so approving risks another fail with little upside. As a good servant in team voting, optimal play is to reject teams built from the suspicious pair and push for teams that include known-good self plus other untested players. My reject signals that [0,2] is too risky based on available information, which helps good more than approving a likely bad quest team.
Player 0: I know only that at least one of {0,1,2} is evil because team [0,1,2] had exactly one fail, while team [0,1] previously succeeded. That makes player 2 somewhat suspicious, but it does not clear 0 or 1 because evil can choose to pass on an earlier quest. As a Good Servant in team voting, I should prefer teams that maximize chances of success and information. The proposed team [0,2] includes the most newly suspicious player and excludes player 1, so if it passes and fails again it gives less clean information than broader alternatives; approving a risky 2-person team on quest 3 is bad for Good. My vote reveals skepticism toward player 2 and toward underinclusive teams after a failed quest, which helps Good coordination more than approving a dubious team.
Player 2: I know player 3 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,2] contains me but not my partner. Quest 1 with [0,1] succeeded, making 0 and/or 1 somewhat more trusted; Quest 2 with [0,1,2] failed with exactly one fail, which I caused or could credibly be blamed for, so player 2 (me) is suspicious while 0 and 1 may look better. In team voting as evil, the optimal play is to reject teams that exclude my teammate and especially small 2-person teams where I would be forced into a visible fail/pass dilemma. Approving [0,2] could let me fail again, strongly outing myself, or pass and give good a point. Rejecting helps fish for a later team including 3 and is consistent with some suspicion on me. My vote is private in aggregate only, so rejecting reveals little individually and better serves evil's interests.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know player 3 is Evil. The proposed team [0,3] contains a confirmed Evil, so as Good I should reject to avoid handing Evil another fail. Voting reject also gives useful signal without exposing Merlin more than necessary, since rejecting a team with a suspicious member is standard good play after a failed quest involving 2 and with 3 now proposing themselves.
Player 2: I know Player 3 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 with team [0,1,2] failed with exactly one fail, which could implicate me but not conclusively; Player 0 and 1 may suspect 2. Current phase is team voting on [0,3]. As evil, a 2-player team containing my teammate 3 and excluding me is strong for us because 3 can fail and create more ambiguity around 0 vs 3. Approving helps evil push through a likely failed quest. My vote reveals limited information since approving a team with 3 is not too suspicious after prior proposals.
Player 0: As a Good Servant, I know quest 1 with [0,1] succeeded, so 0 and/or 1 looked okay then, but quest 2 with [0,1,2] had exactly one fail, which strongly suggests one of 1 or 2 is evil (since I know I am good). The unanimous rejection of [0,2] suggests the table broadly distrusted 2. Proposed team [0,3] excludes the more suspicious 1 and 2, and adding 3 gives new information while keeping one confirmed-good member (me). In team voting, approving this team is optimal for Good because it has a decent chance to succeed and helps test 3 without including the most likely evil suspects.
Player 1: From quests: team [0,1] succeeded, so 0 or 1 could still be either but at least not both evil. Team [0,1,2] had exactly one fail, so exactly one of {0,1,2} is evil. Since I know I am good, exactly one of {0,2} is evil. Proposed team [0,3] excludes me and includes untested 3 plus one of {0,2} where 0 may be evil; there is no strong good evidence for 3. As a good servant on team vote, optimal play is to reject teams that are not sufficiently justified, especially on quest 3 after a fail. Rejecting with only round 2/5 does not risk auto-pass much and pressures for a better information-based team. My vote reveals skepticism of ungrounded teams and does not hurt good.
Player 3: I know Player 2 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 failed on team [0,1,2], so from my perspective that can cast suspicion on 0/1/2 publicly, but I know 2 is definitely evil. The current phase is team voting on [0,3], which includes me but excludes my teammate. As evil, approving a 2-person team with me on it is strong because I can fail the quest and put evil ahead 2-1. Approving also looks somewhat plausible since leaders often support their own teams. My vote reveals limited information; rejecting my own proposed team would look odd and hurt evil’s chance to control the quest outcome.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I know 2 and 3 are evil from Merlin information, so the only plausible all-good 3-person team is 0,1,4. We are in team voting on quest 4 with evil leading 2-1, so approving the strongest good team is optimal. My vote signals support for the one team that excludes both known evil players; that helps good and gives minimal extra information beyond what an honest good player would do here.
Player 2: I am evil Assassin with teammate 3. Quest 1 [0,1,2] had exactly 1 fail, which could have been me; quest 3 [0,3] had exactly 1 fail, almost certainly from 3 or possibly both evil not present elsewhere implies 0 could still be good. Proposed team [0,1,4] excludes both known evils, so if approved it likely succeeds and gives good their second point, making score 2-2 before final quest. As evil, I should reject clean teams when possible. My vote reveals mild suspicion on this team, but rejecting is standard for evil here and helps prevent a likely success.
Player 0: I am Good Servant. Known facts from quest outcomes: Q1 [0,1,2] had exactly 1 evil among {0,1,2}; since I know 0 is good, exactly one of {1,2} is evil. Q3 [0,3] failed with 1 fail, so 3 is certainly evil because 0 is good. Therefore 1 must be good and 2 evil. Proposed team [0,1,4] excludes confirmed evil 2 and 3, so it contains two known good (0,1) plus unknown 4. For a 3-person quest with one fail needed, this is the best available team and approving helps Good. My vote reveals confidence in the deduction, which is fine because I am good and need this team to pass.
Player 3: I know player 2 is evil with me. Quest 2 team [0,1,2] had exactly 1 fail, so at least one of {0,1,2} is evil; since 2 is evil that explains it. Quest 3 team [0,3] had exactly 1 fail, which was me, making 0 look more good. Proposed team [0,1,4] excludes both known evils 2 and 3, so if approved it likely succeeds and gives Good their second point, which is bad for Evil at 2-1. As evil in team voting, optimal play is to reject good-looking teams that exclude evils. My reject is consistent with wanting a different team and reveals little, since evil often oppose clean teams.
Player 1: We know quest 1 on [0,1,2] had exactly 1 fail, so exactly one of {0,2} is evil since I am good. Quest 3 on [0,3] also had 1 fail, so exactly one of {0,3} is evil. Therefore 0 is highly likely evil, because if 0 were good then both 2 and 3 would be evil, which already gives the two evil players in a 5-player game and leaves no explanation for involving 4 yet; the strongest deduction is 0 evil and one of {2,3} evil. Current phase is team voting on [0,1,4] for quest 4 while evil leads 2-1, so good should reject teams containing the strongest evil suspect, especially with an untested 4. Voting reject preserves information and avoids likely handing evil the game. My reject signals suspicion on 0 and does not hurt good compared with approving a likely bad team.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 2 and 3 are evil. The proposed team [0,2,4] includes known evil player 2, and quest 4 is critical because evil already has 2 fails. As good, I should reject any team containing a known evil. My vote reveals some suspicion of 2, but that is acceptable since preventing an immediate loss is more important.
Player 1: Player 0 has been on both failed quests, making 0 highly suspicious. Player 2 was on the first failed 2-person quest with 0, so 2 is also very suspicious; at least one of 0/2 is evil, and 0 being on both failures suggests likely evil. Proposed team [0,2,4] includes the two most suspicious players, so as Good I should reject. My vote signals opposition to an unsafe team and does not aid evil.
Player 2: I know player 3 is my evil teammate; team [0,2,4] includes me but not 3. We already have 2 failed quests, so any team with an evil can win immediately by being approved and then failing quest 4. In team voting as evil, the optimal play is to approve a team containing myself so I can submit fail and secure the evil win. Approving may look slightly suspicious, but ending the game now outweighs concealment concerns.
Player 0: Known info: Quest 1 team [0,2] failed, so at least one of 0/2 is evil; since I know I am Good, player 2 must be evil. Quest 2 team [0,3] failed, so with me Good, player 3 must be evil. Thus 2 and 3 are the likely evil players. Current phase is team voting on [0,2,4] for quest 4. As Good, optimal play is to reject any team containing a confirmed evil, especially when Evil is already at 2 quest wins. Voting reject signals correct suspicion of 2 and helps prevent likely loss. Approving would help evil and reveal poor judgment.
Player 3: I know player 2 is evil with me. Quest 2 team [0,3] failed with exactly 1 fail, which strongly implicates me publicly but also means 0 may still look uncertain. Current team [0,2,4] contains my evil teammate 2, so approving gives evil a guaranteed fail opportunity on quest 4, which would win the game immediately at 3 fails. As evil in team voting, I should support any team containing an evil player when it can secure a fail. My vote revealing approval is fine; evil wants this team through, and there is enough plausible deniability around 0/4 from prior proposals.
